summary: Japan was my homeland where my father stayed and later fell ill. I returned there for an arranged marriage, and we were married at a Shinto shrine by a Shinto priest. Even though I had planned to educate my children in Japan, I ended up raising them in America since I had no relatives there to care for them. During World War II, I sent many desperately needed supplies to our relatives in Japan from America.

When I first arrived in the United States, I stayed with my brother and sister and worked chopping wood. I lived mostly among Japanese people at first and had little contact with Caucasians except for business. After World War II began, my family moved from California to Utah, where I farmed and raised my children. I decided to settle in America after Japan was defeated because this was my children's country and life in Japan seemed difficult after the war.

World War II was a difficult time for us, especially after the Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor. We experienced hardships and had to move from California to Utah. During the war, I sent supplies to relatives in Japan and realized the importance of helping from America. The war also influenced my decision to settle permanently in the United States rather than return to Japan.

I was embarrassed by the Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor, which I understood resulted from American pressure on Japan for oil. This event marked a turning point, leading to suspicion and distrust of Japanese people in America. It also triggered the relocation orders that affected me and many others.

Before moving to Utah, my family lived in California where I faced many hardships. I worked as a farmer but struggled with the bank's high interest rates and poor housing conditions. My eldest son attended UCLA in Los Angeles before transferring to the University of Utah, which helped us legally purchase land in Utah.

After leaving California, we settled in Utah where I bought eleven acres of land and worked on farming, including growing strawberries and maintaining an orchard. We first moved to Springville and worked on a collective farm before moving to Salt Lake City. My eldest son transferred to the University of Utah, which helped us with legal matters related to land purchase.

Los Angeles was where we lived in California and where my eldest son attended UCLA before transferring to the University of Utah. We left Los Angeles due to difficulties and moved to Utah. It was also a place where I had limited interaction with Caucasians except for business matters.

My eldest son transferred from UCLA to the University of Utah, and he played a crucial role in helping us engage a lawyer to buy our property in Utah. The university was important for our family’s transition and settlement in Utah. It also represented a new educational opportunity for my children in America.

My eldest son initially attended UCLA in Los Angeles before transferring to the University of Utah. UCLA was part of our life when we lived in California, but eventually we moved to Utah for better opportunities. His education there was important but the move helped our family settle more securely.

As a Nisei, I faced many challenges during the relocation orders in World War II. I initially thought the notice applied only to Issei, but was disappointed to learn it included all Nisei. I sold my nursery in Seattle at a great loss and prepared for relocation. I believe Nisei would be in a better position if they knew the Japanese language, and I also felt it was unfair to draft Nisei while they were being evacuated and treated as enemies.

I thought the relocation notice during World War II applied only to Issei, the first-generation Japanese immigrants, and was prepared to turn over my nursery to my eldest son. However, I was disappointed to find that the notice included all Nisei as well. This affected my plans and forced me to sell my nursery at a steep loss.

I lived in Seattle during the Great Depression, working as a gardener where my work and income were greatly reduced. I belonged to a church there and was baptized in 1936, learning about God's love. I also operated a nursery in Seattle before the relocation orders forced me to sell it and move. The pastor and church community supported me, though they too faced similar hardships.

During the Great Depression, I was living in Seattle working as a gardener. My work decreased to almost half, and I struggled to pay bills like the telephone. It was a difficult time financially, but I continued to support my family and maintain hope for the future.

My arranged marriage took place at a Shinto shrine, where a Shinto priest officiated our wedding ceremony. After the ceremony, we had a feast to celebrate. This tradition connected me to my Japanese heritage even after moving to America.

I learned about God's love through Christianity and was baptized at a church in Seattle in 1936. The church community, including pastors like Pastor Nicholson, supported me during difficult times such as the relocation. Christianity helped me understand love and neighborly care during challenging periods.

After relocation, we moved to Springville, Utah, where I worked on a collective farm. This was a new and challenging experience, but it allowed us to continue farming and supporting our family. Later, we moved to Salt Lake City for better opportunities.

Eventually, we settled in Salt Lake City, Utah, where I cultivated strawberries and grew fruits. This became our permanent home, and I was able to enjoy a retired life there. Salt Lake City provided a place for my family to thrive after many hardships.
